topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notification sat on my desk, crisp and unforgiving. The scholarship. It was mine. I traced the embossed lettering with a fingertip, the paper feeling suddenly cold beneath my touch. Every line, every seal, seemed to scream a betrayal. I reread the fine print, the clauses about eligibility, the names listed in the appendix. My breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air. It wasn't my name. It belonged to someone else entirely. A sudden, prickly heat rose up my neck, making the fine hairs on my arms stand on end. I pushed the papers away, the sound of the slight scrape echoing too loudly in the quiet room. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space between my chair and the window, my jaw tight. I needed to verify this. I needed to know who they were talking about, and I started pulling up old departmental records, my fingers moving with a focused, almost frantic energy.

[story 2]

I kept glancing at the empty chair across the lecture hall, half-expecting someone to materialize and correct this glaring error. The acceptance letter felt like a carefully constructed trap. When I finally managed to decipher the discrepancy—the name on the award was completely wrong—a strange, tight knot formed just beneath my sternum. My hands clenched into fists in my lap, the knuckles turning white. I didn't look at the professor; I focused instead on the details of the application packet, scrutinizing every signature, every date. A low, almost inaudible hum vibrated in my ears, a constant reminder that something was fundamentally skewed. I spent the next hour cross-referencing data, my movements economical and precise, driven by an intense need to untangle this mess before it became irrevocably tangled.

[story 3]

The envelope was sealed with wax, heavy and official. Opening it felt like cracking open a fragile shell that held a terrible secret. My eyes scanned the text, and the realization hit me not as a sudden shock, but as a slow, chilling realization that spread through my chest like icy water. It wasn't mine. It was designated for another applicant. A tremor ran through my hands as I dropped the letter onto the wooden surface of my desk. I remained perfectly still for a long moment, listening to the silence of the apartment, my gaze darting around the room as if searching for an unseen observer. A slow, deliberate sweep of my eyes moved from the door to the window, a silent inventory of the surroundings, as if ensuring no one had witnessed this unwelcome discovery.
